FT_IC1.evidence: Paper analyzes U.S. ABCD Study data; explicitly states "Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD; Baseline N = 11,878)" with recruitment from 21 U.S. sites; uses 4 waves of longitudinal data (Years 1–4).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; multigroup latent growth curve models examining parent-child reporting discrepancy across smartphone ownership age groups; reports direct effects, interactions, and longitudinal patterns with test statistics (p values, confidence intervals).

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed published article in Development and Psychopathology (2025).

FT_IC2.evidence: Smartphone ownership (yes/no, age at first ownership) qualifies as smartphone exposure. Individual-trait-level outcome: parent-child discrepancy in reporting youth internalizing problems (mental health). Internalizing problems are within-person behavioral/mental health constructs. Relationship: smartphone ownership → internalizing problems discrepancy.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric quantitative results reported extensively: regression coefficients (b = −0.21, SE = .042, p < .001 for informant effect across full sample; Table 3 with parameter estimates and p-values for all groups; latent growth curve slope estimates with 95% CIs). Figure 1 shows multigroup results with standardized coefficients.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis with quantitative results: multilevel models with random effects, fixed effects tests (χ²), significance tests, confidence intervals, and effect estimates at multiple timepoints. Direct effects of time and informant on internalizing problems reported with test statistics.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional and longitudinal design where smartphone is treated as a predictor (IV), not only a DV. The paper examines how smartphone ownership predicts (is IV) to changes in parent-child discrepancy in internalizing problems (DV), and also examines discrepancy as a moderator of other effects. Not DV-only.
notes: Strong paper examining smartphone ownership as a novel longitudinal predictor of parent-child informant discrepancy in mental health outcomes. Uses advanced statistical methods (multilevel modeling, latent growth curves).

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper uses U.S. ABCD Study data; explicitly states "ABCD study (T1; N = 11,787)" and "data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development Study" with 3-year longitudinal follow-up.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; cross-lagged panel models examining bidirectional associations between electronic media use and family conflict across 3 waves; reports regression coefficients, standardized estimates, and multimodel comparisons with fit indices (CFI, TLI, RMSEA).

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Journal of Family Psychology (2025).

FT_IC2.evidence: Electronic media (EMU) qualifying variables: total electronic media use, social electronic media use (text content specifies examining specific types of media including social platforms). Individual-trait-level outcome: family conflict (behavioral/family-level construct; family members negotiating conflict, disagreement, discord). Relationships: EMU (both total and social-specific) as IV predicting later family conflict; bidirectional associations examined. Social media explicitly isolated as a distinct media type with separate analyses.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric quantitative results reported: standardized regression coefficients in cross-lagged models (β, p-values; e.g., β = 0.10 for overall EMU predicting family conflict; Table 1 shows means, SDs, and correlations; path estimates reported with significance indicators). Figure 1 and Figure 2 display path coefficients.

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis using cross-lagged panel models; multiple timepoints tested (T1–T4); comparative model fit indices reported (CFI, TLI, RMSEA); statistical significance testing throughout. Multiple models tested by media type and gender; sensitivity analyses conducted.

FT_EC7_reason: Bidirectional design: electronic media is both IV (predicting later family conflict) and DV (family conflict predicting later media use). Additionally, design is examined across multiple waves in both directions, not SM-as-DV-only.
notes: Well-designed longitudinal bidirectional study examining how overall electronic media use and specific social media use relate to family conflict. Examines specificity by media type (social EMU, video viewing, video games) and gender differences.

FT_IC1.evidence: Paper uses U.S. ABCD Study data; states "data from the ABCD Study (Baseline N ~ 12,000; Mage = 9.9; White = 52.0%, Hispanic = 20.3%, Black = 15.0%, Asian = 2.1%, Other = 10.5%; Female = 47.8%)" with 4-year follow-up from early adolescence.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis; parallel process latent growth curve models testing whether growth trajectories in social media use are associated with trajectories in cognitive abilities (crystallized, inhibitory control/attention, processing speed) across 4 waves.

FT_IC6.evidence: Peer-reviewed article in Journal of Research on Adolescence (2025).

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use (time spent on social media platforms; self-reported daily hours weekday and weekend; coded as weighted average; youth reported on SM use habits asking about timespan and platforms) qualifies as social media exposure. Individual-trait-level outcomes: crystallized abilities (reading, vocabulary knowledge), inhibitory control/attention (Flanker task performance), processing speed (Pattern Comparison Task). All are cognitive/individual-trait constructs. Relationship: growth trajectories in social media use coupled with (linked to) cognitive performance trajectories across early adolescence.

FT_IC3.evidence: Numeric quantitative results extensively reported: latent growth curve parameter estimates (intercepts, slopes) with standard errors and confidence intervals for each cognitive domain (Table 2: social media intercept = 0.11 [95% CI 0.10, 0.12], slope = 0.36 [0.35, 0.38]; crystallized abilities intercept = 87.61, slope = 2.13 with p < .001). Parallel process model covariances between SM and cognitive trajectories reported (e.g., slope-slope associations with β, SE, p-values in Table 3).

FT_IC4.evidence: Inferential analysis using parallel process latent growth curve models; intercept-slope covariances tested; cross-construct associations reported with parameter estimates, standard errors, and p-values. Model fit indices excellent (CFI = 0.99, TLI = 0.97, RMSA = 0.03). Sensitivity analyses conducted using listwise deletion.

FT_EC7_reason: Longitudinal design but social media is not DV-only. Paper tests whether SM trajectories are coupled with (predict/associate with) cognitive ability trajectories, positioning SM as an IV-type predictor of cognitive growth patterns. Cross-construct associations are bidirectional in concept (both could influence the other), not unidirectional DV.
notes: Sophisticated study using parallel process latent growth curve modeling to examine domain-specific coupling between social media trajectories and cognitive ability trajectories. Finds negative coupling with crystallized and inhibitory control/attention but positive with processing speed.
